ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: Iowa 2026 primary election. Polls open 7:00 AM – 8:00 PM Central Time. Iowa is a closed primary — voters must be registered with a party.
Pre-registration deadline for June 2 primary (15 days before election, by 5:00 PM CT). Same-day registration available May 19 through June 2 with photo ID and proof of residence.
Deadline for mailed absentee ballot requests to reach the county auditor by 5:00 PM CT. In-person absentee voting available through June 1.
Absentee ballots must be received by the county auditor by 8:00 PM CT on election day. Postmarks do not count.
Iowa 2026 general election. Polls open 7:00 AM – 8:00 PM Central Time.
Pre-registration deadline for November 3 general election (15 days before, by 5:00 PM CT). Same-day registration available Oct 20 through Nov 3 with proof of ID and residence.
Deadline for mailed absentee ballot requests to reach the county auditor by 5:00 PM CT. In-person absentee voting available through November 2.
Absentee ballots must be received by the county auditor by 8:00 PM CT on election day. Postmarks do not count.

ELECTION_EVENTS.milestone: Last day for voters to fix incomplete affidavit envelopes
ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: Voters have until the time polls close at 8:00 p.m. CT to fix incomplete affidavit envelopes at the county auditor's office.
Iowa holds a closed primary. Voters must be registered with a party to participate. Independents may change party registration on election day at the polls.

RULES.currentRule: Online, in person, or by mail. Must be a U.S. citizen, Iowa resident, 18+ by election day. Pre-registration deadline is 15 days before the election. Same-day registration is available through election day with photo ID and proof of residence.
RULES.whyItMatters: Determines who can vote in IA-01. Voters in all 20 counties must register with their county auditor — Scott (Davenport), Johnson (Iowa City), and others.
RULES.detail: Primary pre-reg deadline: May 18, 2026 at 5 PM. General pre-reg deadline: October 19, 2026 at 5 PM. Same-day registration available with photo ID + proof of current address.

RULES.currentRule: Iowa HF 954 (signed June 2025) strengthens citizenship verification at voter registration — cross-referencing DOT noncitizen ID files. Registrants flagged as potential non-citizens are placed in 'unconfirmed' status; they must provide evidence of citizenship to vote a full ballot.
RULES.whyItMatters: New 2025 law adds an active citizenship cross-check at registration. Voters with 'unconfirmed' status may need to provide documentation before casting a full ballot.
RULES.detail: Iowa HF 954 took effect in 2025. Monitor for county auditor implementation — especially in high-population counties like Scott and Johnson.

RULES.currentRule: Iowa requires photo ID to vote in person. Acceptable IDs: Iowa driver's license, Iowa non-operator ID, U.S. passport, military/veteran ID, tribal ID, or Iowa Voter ID card (must be signed). ID must not be expired more than 90 days for driver's licenses.
RULES.whyItMatters: Voters without a qualifying ID will need another registered voter in the same precinct to attest to their identity, or they may cast a provisional ballot.
RULES.detail: Iowa Voter ID cards are issued automatically to registered voters who lack an Iowa driver's license or non-operator ID. Any voter may request one from their county auditor.

RULES.currentRule: Any registered voter may request an absentee ballot — no excuse required. Request must include Iowa driver's license number, non-operator ID number, voter ID/verification number, or 4-digit PIN from voter ID card.
RULES.whyItMatters: Absentee voting is widely used in IA-01 counties. Requests must be submitted by the county auditor deadline; ballots must be received — not postmarked — by 8 PM on election day.

RULES.currentRule: Mailed absentee ballot requests must be received by the county auditor by 5:00 PM, 15 days before the election. Primary deadline: May 18, 2026. General deadline: October 19, 2026. In-person requests may be made through the day before the election.
RULES.whyItMatters: Late mailed requests are rejected. Voters who miss the mail deadline may still request an absentee ballot in person at the county auditor's office through the day before the election.

RULES.currentRule: In-person absentee voting begins 20 days before the election at each county auditor's office. Primary early voting window: May 13 – June 1, 2026. General window: October 14 – November 2, 2026.
RULES.whyItMatters: Iowa's early voting mechanism is the 'in-person absentee' system — voters visit the county auditor's office, request and cast their ballot in one step.

RULES.currentRule: Polls are open 7:00 AM to 8:00 PM Central Time on election day. Any voter in line at 8:00 PM is entitled to vote. Voters must go to their assigned precinct — Iowa does not use open vote centers.
RULES.whyItMatters: IA-01 counties use precinct-based voting. Voting at the wrong precinct results in a provisional ballot that may not count for all races.

RULES.currentRule: Iowa holds a closed primary — voters must be registered with a party to vote in that party's primary. Independents and unaffiliated voters must declare a party affiliation to participate. Party registration can be changed on election day.
RULES.whyItMatters: Unaffiliated voters who arrive at the polls cannot vote in either party's primary without first declaring a party. They may change party on election day at the polling place.

RULES.currentRule: Absentee ballots must be physically received by the county auditor by 8:00 PM on election day. Postmarks do not count. Primary receipt deadline: June 2, 2026 at 8 PM. General: November 3, 2026 at 8 PM.
RULES.whyItMatters: Most common cause of absentee ballot rejection in Iowa. Mail transit time in rural IA-01 counties can be unreliable — return by drop-off at the county auditor to be safe.
RULES.detail: Iowa Code § 53. Ballots may be returned in person to the county auditor by any person authorized by the voter, including a family member.

RULES.currentRule: Common rejection causes: late receipt, missing or invalid voter verification number on request form, signature mismatch, voter not registered at address on file.
RULES.whyItMatters: Rejected absentee ballots are not counted. Iowa does not have a broad cure process comparable to other states — voters should verify receipt before election day.

RULES.currentRule: Voters who lack required ID, are not in the precinct roster, or whose registration is in 'unconfirmed' or 'inactive' status may cast a provisional ballot. Provisional ballots are counted after identity and eligibility are confirmed.
RULES.whyItMatters: Affects IA-01 voters who lack ID, have recently moved, or face registration issues under the new HF 954 citizenship-verification rules.

RULES.currentRule: County canvass is held within 10 days after the election. State canvass and certification occurs not later than 27 days after election day (Iowa Code § 50.38).
RULES.whyItMatters: Determines when IA-01 race results become official. The Secretary of State presents vote abstracts to the Board of State Canvassers, which certifies results.
RULES.detail: General election state canvass: not later than December 1, 2026 (Iowa Code § 50.38(c)).

RULES.currentRule: Iowa HF 928 (2025) reformed the recount process. For federal offices, a recount may be requested if the margin is less than 0.15%. Recounts must use election-day tabulation machines, cover all precincts, and be conducted by county auditors with bipartisan boards. Secretary of State may take over a recount if board is not following the law.
RULES.whyItMatters: Highly relevant for IA-01: Rep. Miller-Meeks won by fewer than 6 votes in 2020 and by less than 1% in 2024. The new 0.15% threshold and SOS takeover authority are active for 2026.
RULES.detail: HF 928 signed June 2, 2025. Recount requests may be filed within 3 days of county canvass. State covers cost if margin is within 0.15% for federal races. Completion deadline: 18 days after county canvass.

UPDATES.title: HF 928 recount reform now in effect for 2026
UPDATES.fieldChanged: Recount eligibility threshold for federal offices
UPDATES.oldValue: No automatic recount; candidate-requested with no statutory margin threshold
UPDATES.newValue: Recount may be requested if margin < 0.15% for federal races (HF 928, eff. July 1, 2025)

UPDATES.title: HF 954 citizenship verification active at registration
UPDATES.newValue: Cross-reference with DOT noncitizen ID files; unconfirmed-status voters must provide documentation (HF 954, eff. 2025)

UPDATES.title: Dashboard pivot: primary deadlines past, general-election focus active
UPDATES.oldValue: KPI cards led with primary registration and absentee deadlines (May 18, June 2)
UPDATES.newValue: KPI cards now lead with general-election deadlines (Oct 19 registration, Oct 14 – Nov 2 in-person early voting, Nov 3 Election Day and 8 PM CT ballot receipt). Miller-Meeks (R) vs. Bohannan (D) is the confirmed November 3 rematch.

UPDATES.title: General-election deadlines re-checked; no changes detected
UPDATES.oldValue: Deadlines confirmed as of 2026-05-27 initial build.
UPDATES.newValue: As of the June 17, 2026 refresh, Iowa Secretary of State and Johnson County auditor guidance still list October 19, 2026 (5:00 PM CT) as the pre-registration deadline, October 19, 2026 (5:00 PM CT) as the mailed-absentee-request deadline, October 14 – November 2, 2026 as the in-person early-voting window, and November 3, 2026 at 8:00 PM CT as Election Day and the absentee-ballot receipt deadline. Postmarks do not count. Election-day registration remains available with photo ID and proof of residence.

CONFLICTS.factInQuestion: Same-day registration: which ID documents qualify
CONFLICTS.sourceAClaim: Official guide states photo ID + proof of current address required for same-day registration.
CONFLICTS.sourceBClaim: County FAQ notes that proof of residence may be satisfied by various document types; exact list should be confirmed with the county auditor.
CONFLICTS.whyItMatters: Voters attempting to register on election day in IA-01 need to know exactly what documents to bring.
CONFLICTS.suggestedReviewAction: Verify the specific same-day registration document list with Scott and Johnson county auditors before the June 2 primary.

NEWS.headline: Iowa primary election day: Miller-Meeks faces Pautsch, Bohannan faces Terrell
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Polls open June 2 (7 AM–8 PM CT) for Iowa's primary. Miller-Meeks (R) faces David Pautsch, who won 44% in the 2024 primary. Bohannan (D) faces first-time candidate Travis Terrell. Winners advance to the November 3 general election in this Cook-rated toss-up.

NEWS.headline: Democrats are eyeing Iowa's 1st Congressional District as a seat that can be flipped
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Democrats see IA-01 as a key flip opportunity. Christina Bohannan (former state rep, 2022/2024 nominee) faces Travis Terrell (healthcare worker) in the Democratic primary. Republican incumbent Miller-Meeks faces primary challenger David Pautsch. Cook Political Report rates IA-01 a toss-up.

NEWS.headline: Miller-Meeks faces primary challenger David Pautsch in June Republican contest
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Rep. Mariannette Miller-Meeks faces a primary challenge from David Pautsch, a ministry founder and MAGA-aligned Republican from Davenport who also ran in 2024. Miller-Meeks won the 2024 general by less than 1 point — the narrowest margin of any House Republican that cycle.

NEWS.headline: Independent candidate Michael Bridgford enters 'toss-up' race for Iowa's 1st Congressional District
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Independent Michael Bridgford (financial advisor, Bettendorf) entered IA-01 to challenge both parties. He is gathering signatures to qualify for the November ballot and plans a 20-county tour. His candidacy adds a third option in a race Cook rates as a toss-up.

NEWS.headline: Iowa recount process gets major overhaul under HF 928
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Iowa's new recount law (HF 928, signed June 2025) sets a 0.15% margin threshold for federal race recounts, requires all-precinct machine recounts, and gives the Secretary of State authority to take over a recount if county boards don't follow the law — highly relevant given IA-01's history of razor-thin margins.

NEWS.headline: Iowa bans ranked-choice voting, strengthens citizenship checks under HF 954
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Gov. Reynolds signed HF 954 banning ranked-choice voting statewide and requiring citizenship verification at voter registration through DOT database cross-checks. The Brennan Center flagged Iowa's new SOS recount-takeover authority as among 2025's most significant election-interference laws.

LAST_REFRESH.summary: Automated refresh completed 2026-07-03 for IA-01. Sources checked: 15. LLM verifications: 17. 2 lastVerified bumps on unchanged sources.

WEEKLY_REPORT.summary: Weekly refresh for week of June 11–17, 2026. Dashboard narrative pivoted from pre-primary deadlines to post-primary, general-election focus. June 2 primary is past — Miller-Meeks (R) defeated Pautsch; Bohannan (D) defeated Terrell. The Miller-Meeks vs. Bohannan rematch is confirmed for November 3 (Cook: toss-up). Re-checked Iowa SOS and Johnson County calendars — general-election deadlines unchanged (Oct. 19 registration, Oct. 19 mail-absentee request, Oct. 14 – Nov. 2 early voting, Nov. 3 Election Day and 8:00 PM CT receipt). Watch items unchanged: HF 928 recount reform (0.15% federal threshold; SOS takeover authority) and HF 954 citizenship verification both still on 'watch' with needsReview:true. conflict-ia01-001 (same-day registration document list for Scott and Johnson counties) remains open. No confidence flips — all rules await owner verification before publish.
